name: CapRover Self-Hosted PaaS API
what_it_does: Open-source self-hosted PaaS with REST API — enabling developers and teams to deploy applications via Docker on their own servers using Docker Swarm for clustering, with a web dashboard, one-click app templates (100+ apps), automatic SSL, load balancing, CI/CD webhook triggers, and git-push deployment support.
use_cases: Deploying containerized applications via CapRover REST API for self-hosted Docker deployment automation
Managing CapRover app configurations and environment variables via API for deployment pipeline
Triggering CapRover deployments via API webhooks for CI/CD integration
Provisioning one-click CapRover apps (databases, services) via API for self-hosted service setup
Managing CapRover worker nodes via API for Docker Swarm cluster expansion
not_for: Teams without server administration skills (CapRover requires your own server)
Enterprise workloads requiring high-availability beyond Docker Swarm
Teams wanting fully managed infrastructure (CapRover is self-hosted)
best_when: A developer or team wants a beautiful web UI with one-click app templates for self-hosted deployment on their own server or VPS — with Docker Swarm clustering and CI/CD webhook integration.
avoid_when: You need managed infrastructure, Kubernetes-grade orchestration, or don't have server administration skills.

auth.notes: CapRover API uses bearer token from login endpoint. POST /api/v2/login to get token with password. Token-based auth for all subsequent requests. No OAuth2. No fine-grained scopes — single admin token provides full access. Webhook for CI/CD deployment triggers (token in webhook URL). App-specific deployment tokens for CI/CD (narrower than admin token).

pricing.free_tier_limits: Completely free and open source; only server costs
pricing.paid_tiers: No paid tiers — community donations supported
pricing.notes: Open source project. Created by Behzad Shirani. Apache 2.0 license. 13,000+ GitHub stars. React frontend, Node.js backend. Docker Swarm for clustering (not Kubernetes). One-click app library: WordPress, Ghost, MongoDB, MySQL, Redis, etc. Automatic SSL via Let's Encrypt. Git push deployment via Captain CLI. CI/CD via webhook with app-specific tokens. Very popular Heroku alternative in self-hosting community. Works on DigitalOcean, Linode, Hetzner, Vultr, etc.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST API documented with OpenAPI. Token-based auth (login to get token). No rate limits (self-hosted). Webhook for CI/CD deployments. Requires server access for setup. Open source — can inspect API source. Good documentation for self-hosting community. App-specific deployment tokens enable scoped CI/CD integration.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: App reads idempotent by app name. Environment variable sets idempotent by key. Deployment triggers not idempotent. Scale operations idempotent by count.
known_agent_gotchas: Token obtained via POST /api/v2/login — must authenticate first to get bearer token
Admin token provides full system access — use app-specific tokens for CI/CD
Docker Swarm not Kubernetes — different from EKS/GKE deployments
Self-hosted — agents need network access to the CapRover server
No rate limits but server resources are shared — throttle heavy operations

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced for CapRover dashboard. User-controlled data residency. Password-based token auth — enforce strong password and token expiry. App-specific tokens for CI/CD provide some scope narrowing. Let's Encrypt for automatic SSL. Docker Swarm secrets for sensitive data. Open source — auditable. User responsible for server hardening. No compliance certifications.
